answer: The relationship between cetana (Conscious Principle) and acetana (Unconscious Principle) is defined in Vrhaspatitattva, Chapter 1, around verse 6 (paragraphs p-04d69c and p-c31df3). The text states: 'The supreme tattva is twofold: cetana and acetana. It pervades all tattvas, is subtle, and must be discerned with effort.' (verse 6). The prose exposition defines cetana as 'that which has the nature of knowledge, which knows and is never subject to forgetting, which is eternally aware at all times, and which is unobscured.' Acetana is defined as 'that which is without knowledge, like a stone.' Their relationship is generative: 'When cetana and acetana meet, they give rise to all the tattvas' — specifically pradhānatattva, triguṇatattva, buddhitattva, ahaṅkāratattva, bāhyendriyatattva, karmendriyatattva, and pañcamahābhūtatattva (the sarvatattva). Cetana is further subdivided into three types: Paramaśivatattva, Sadāśivatattva, and Śivatattva. The text also later clarifies (paragraph around para 144) that 'what is called unconscious is willed into being by the conscious — it is like clay being made [into a pot],' underscoring the ontological primacy of cetana over acetana.
